title: Nvidia Concedes China's AI Chip Market to Huawei as Subsea Cable Coercion Intensifies
subhead: Iran demands fees for undersea cables in the Strait of Hormuz, while Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang acknowledges the company has 'largely conceded' China's AI chip market to Huawei. Taiwan cracks down on chip smuggling, and Russia stages nuclear drills near NATO borders.

sections.heading: Iran Claims Authority Over Hormuz's Subsea Internet Cables
sections.body:
Iran's military and Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps spokesperson Ebrahim Zolfaghari announced Tehran will impose fees on undersea internet cables passing through the Strait of Hormuz, according to Iranian state media. Tasnim and Fars news outlets detailed proposals to charge license fees to Meta, Google, Amazon, and Microsoft for cable usage, while claiming Iran alone has rights to repair and maintain cables in the strait.

The threats come amid an ongoing conflict that has already halted new cable projects and suspended repairs in the region. French state-owned Alcatel Submarine Networks notified customers in March that it could not fulfill contracts due to a cable-laying ship stranded near Saudi Arabia, suspending a Meta-backed undersea cable project. TeleGeography estimates the major active cables through Hormuz—Asia Africa Europe-1, FALCON, and Gulf Bridge International—primarily serve Gulf countries, with limited impact on Europe-Asia traffic that routes through the Red Sea.

Windward, a maritime intelligence company, warned that operators face a choice: 'pay protection fees and accept Iranian licensing over Middle East Gulf seabed activity, or accept that future faults may go unrepaired indefinitely.' The US military claims to have destroyed 161 Iranian naval vessels since the conflict began in February, but Iran retains asymmetric leverage over digital infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is extending its Hormuz blockade strategy from oil to digital infrastructure, creating a new category of economic coercion. The threat of unrepairable cable damage could accelerate alternative routing projects and push Gulf countries toward overland fiber connections.

sections.heading: Nvidia CEO: 'We've Largely Conceded' China's AI Chip Market to Huawei
sections.body:
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang acknowledged the company has 'largely conceded' China's advanced AI chip market to Huawei, marking a dramatic shift from the company's former position. Nvidia once controlled over 90% of China's AI chip market; that figure has collapsed to near zero following US export controls that began in 2022.

The retreat has created space for Huawei's Ascend AI chips, which now power increasing portions of China's domestic AI workloads. According to Morgan Stanley data reported by Bloomberg, China's AI chip self-sufficiency ratio reached 41% this year—four times higher than five years ago—and is projected to hit 85% by 2028.

The acknowledgment comes despite the Trump administration approving renewed Nvidia H200 GPU shipments to China last week after Trump's meeting with President Xi Jinping. Chinese tech giants including Alibaba, JD.com, Tencent, and ByteDance were reportedly cleared for purchases. But Beijing appears reluctant to reopen floodgates after years of pushing domestic suppliers. Huawei and Chinese cloud providers have adapted software stacks around local hardware, reducing dependency on Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem.
sections.whyItMatters: US export controls designed to slow China's AI progress may have accelerated its semiconductor independence instead. The 85% self-sufficiency projection fundamentally changes the leverage equation—Beijing now has alternatives it lacked three years ago.

sections.heading: Taiwan Cracks Down on Chip Smuggling in First Major Enforcement Action
sections.body:
Taiwanese prosecutors are seeking to detain three individuals for forging documents to export Nvidia AI chips to China, marking Taipei's first major enforcement action against semiconductor smuggling. The defendants allegedly made fraudulent declarations about AI servers manufactured by US-based Super Micro Computer to ship hardware to China, Hong Kong, and Macau in violation of US trade rules.

The volume is relatively small—approximately 50 servers—but the case signals a shift under President Lai Ching-te's administration. Taiwan has faced years of US pressure to strengthen enforcement against export control violations occurring on the island, which hosts the vast majority of advanced chip manufacturing.

The action follows the largest chip smuggling prosecution in the US, where Super Micro's co-founder was indicted for allegedly diverting billions of dollars worth of Nvidia chips to China. Taiwan's Keelung District Prosecutors Office said the case was initiated independently and is not directly linked to the US indictment, though investigations continue into possible connections.

Taiwan is using local fraud laws rather than matching US export controls directly—a similar approach to Singapore's 2025 arrests of individuals who misled server suppliers about ultimate destinations. The move indicates Taiwan's willingness to protect its technological edge without adopting Washington's full sanctions regime.
sections.whyItMatters: Taiwan's first major smuggling enforcement shows Lai's government responding to US pressure while protecting its chip ecosystem. The case highlights how export controls create smuggling incentives even in Taiwan, where most advanced chips are manufactured.

sections.heading: Samsung Averts Strike That Could Have Disrupted Global Memory Supply
sections.body:
Samsung Electronics' labor union suspended a planned 18-day strike after reaching a tentative wage deal with management, averting disruption at the world's largest memory chip maker. The union, representing over 70,000 workers, agreed to put the agreement to a vote from May 22-27.

South Korean government officials had threatened to invoke emergency powers to force a settlement, warning the strike could cause up to 100 trillion won ($66 billion) in economic damage. Samsung and rival SK Hynix together produce about two-thirds of the world's memory chips, which face surging demand from AI infrastructure investment.

Samsung reported record operating profit of 57.2 trillion won ($38 billion) for the January-March quarter—an eightfold increase driven by AI chip demand. The union had demanded Samsung commit 15% of annual operating profit to employee bonuses and scrap bonus caps. Management cited the semiconductor industry's cyclical nature in resisting those demands, but signaled willingness to extend bonuses beyond the memory division to less profitable units.
sections.whyItMatters: A prolonged Samsung strike would have tightened global memory supply and potentially delayed AI infrastructure investments. The settlement removes immediate risk but highlights labor tension in a sector critical to global tech infrastructure.

sections.heading: Russia Stages Nuclear Drills with Belarus as Ukrainian Drone Attacks Intensify
sections.body:
Russia and Belarus concluded joint nuclear drills involving 64,000 troops, over 200 missile launchers, 140 aircraft, and 13 submarines, including eight armed with nuclear-tipped ICBMs. The three-day exercise, which ended Thursday, practiced 'preparation and use of nuclear forces under the threat of aggression,' according to Russia's Defense Ministry.

The drills featured Yars and Sineva ICBM test launches, along with medium-range Zircon and Kinzhal missiles. Belarusian troops test-fired short-range Iskander ballistic missiles inside Russia. Moscow has deployed nuclear-capable Oreshnik intermediate-range missile systems to Belarus, which borders Ukraine and NATO members Poland, Latvia, and Lithuania.

President Putin emphasized that 'the use of nuclear weapons is an extreme, exceptional measure' while discussing the drills with Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko. The maneuvers come amid increased Ukrainian drone strikes on Moscow's suburbs that killed three people and damaged buildings this week. On Tuesday, a NATO jet shot down a Ukrainian drone that entered Estonian airspace—highlighting the conflict's spillover risks.

Russia's revised 2024 nuclear doctrine places Belarus under Moscow's nuclear umbrella and states that any conventional attack supported by a nuclear power will be considered a joint attack—a clear warning about Western support for Ukraine.
sections.whyItMatters: The drills reinforce nuclear signaling as Ukraine escalates drone attacks on Russian territory. The NATO interception of a Ukrainian drone in Estonia shows how the conflict creates risks of unintended escalation with alliance members.

sections.heading: Türkiye-Japan Defense Partnership Targets Drone Technology
sections.body:
Türkiye and Japan signed a letter of intent to deepen defense industry cooperation during the first-ever Turkey-Japan Defense Industry Cooperation Day in Istanbul on Wednesday. Thirty Turkish companies—including drone manufacturer Baykar—and 10 Japanese defense firms attended the event at SAHA 2026, one of Türkiye's largest arms fairs.

Türkiye's Presidency of Defense Industries (SSB) head Haluk Gorgun said 'important steps were taken toward strategic cooperation in defense industry and high-technology fields.' Japan's ambassador to Ankara, Masami Tamura, called the event 'an important step' for bilateral defense cooperation.

Japanese news outlets reported Tokyo's particular interest in Baykar's TB2 combat performance in Ukraine and Libya. Japan's 2026 defense budget earmarks roughly $70 million for its first five 'wide-area' UAVs—long-endurance drones intended to replace some reconnaissance and attack functions of the country's retiring helicopter fleet.

Türkiye became the world's 11th-largest arms exporter in the 2021-2025 period after exports surged 122% compared to the previous five years, according to SIPRI data. Baykar signed a record export deal involving Bayraktar Akinci UAVs with Saudi defense firms in 2023, and established a joint venture with Italy's Leonardo in 2025 for European market production.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is diversifying drone suppliers beyond US and Israeli platforms, while Türkiye seeks technology partnerships that strengthen its defense industrial base. The partnership reflects how mid-tier defense exporters are forming new technology alliances outside traditional US-NATO channels.

sections.heading: Taiwan Cable Cuts Raise Sabotage Questions
sections.body:
Submarine cables connecting Taiwan's outlying Matsu islands were cut for the third time in four years in late April, affecting 1,500 residents of Dongyin Township. The incident highlights Taiwan's infrastructure vulnerability given more than 20 cable cuts in the last five years.

International reporting attributed the cut to a shipwreck—the Chinese vessel Min Lian Yu 63896, abandoned at sea on March 21. But Taiwanese media highlighted unusual patterns from the salvage vessel Hai Hong Gong 66, commissioned by the wreck's owner. The vessel took a meandering route around Matsu rather than heading directly to the wreck, passing near submarine cable infrastructure.

The Min Lian Yu 63896 previously participated in civilian-military fusion exercises—the same type of operations used to test quarantine maneuvers around Taiwan. China has patented technology for undersea cable cutting, though most alleged sabotage involves ships dragging anchors over cables.

Repair vessels have been unable to access the Strait of Hormuz since the Iran conflict began. Similar delays in Taiwan Strait could leave outlying islands disconnected for extended periods.
sections.whyItMatters: Taiwan's repeated cable cuts and the difficulty of attribution—whether caused by weather, accidents, or deliberate sabotage—illustrate how infrastructure disruption can create pressure without triggering direct conflict. The island's digital connectivity is increasingly contested.

bottomLine: Iran extended its Hormuz blockade strategy from oil to digital infrastructure by demanding fees for undersea cables, while Nvidia formally acknowledged losing China's AI chip market to Huawei—a market it once dominated with over 90% share. Taiwan's first major smuggling enforcement shows export controls creating black-market incentives, and Samsung averted a strike that could have tightened global memory supply. Russia's nuclear drills with Belarus signal escalation risks as Ukrainian drones reach Moscow and NATO airspace. Japan's interest in Turkish drone technology reflects new defense-industrial partnerships forming outside traditional US-NATO channels.
sources: Ars Technica, Associated Press, Reuters, Straits Times, Kyiv Independent, The Diplomat, Al-Monitor, AP News, 24/7 Wall St., Military.com, Nikkei Asia, CNN, The Guardian, TeleGeography, Windward, Morgan Stanley via Bloomberg
